Economic Intelligence › Economy › Gateshead Productivity & GVA

Gateshead — Productivity & Gross Value Added

Where the borough's £5bn economy is growing, where it is at risk, its biggest sectors, and how its productivity benchmarks against the North East, the UK, and Gateshead's ONS economic statistical neighbours. Built on ONS balanced GVA and subregional productivity, enriched with NOMIS business and employment counts.

Two decades of relative decline

Gateshead productivity as a share of the UK average (UK = 100). A falling line means slower growth than the UK — not necessarily a fall in absolute terms.

Nominal vs real GVA — growth is mostly inflation

Current-price GVA (cash) against chained-volume GVA (real, 2022 money). The gap is price inflation.

Key findings

GVA: current prices, real terms & growth

Balanced GVA measures the value added by all industries in the borough. Current prices include inflation; chained-volume measures (CVM) strip it out, so they are the right lens for real growth over time.

Current prices vs chained volume (real)

£ million. Real GVA peaked in the mid-2000s and has stagnated since.

Real GVA growth, indexed — Gateshead vs NE vs UK

Chained-volume GVA rebased to 1998 = 100.

Real growth by period

% change in chained-volume GVA over each window.

What this means

    Sectoral structure

    Gateshead's industry mix by gross value added, how it has shifted, and how concentrated — and therefore exposed — the economy is. Section letters follow SIC 2007.

    Biggest sectors by GVA

    Real growth by sector

    Change in chained-volume GVA over the last decade. Green = growing, red = shrinking in real terms.

    Sector mix vs UK & North East

    Share of total GVA. Gateshead is over-weight in public-facing services and under-weight in high-value private services.

    Sector value per job

    GVA (2023) ÷ employee jobs (BRES) by sector, Gateshead vs England. Shows which sectors create the most value per worker.

    How concentrated is the economy?

    Sector share of GVA over time — the changing shape of the economy.

    Business base (UKBC, )

    Gateshead's businesses by size and by sector. A small-firm economy.

    Labour productivity

    Output per hour worked and per filled job — the headline measures of how efficiently the economy turns work into value. Smoothed, current-price, ONS subregional series.

    GVA per hour worked (£)

    Gateshead vs the North East average and the UK.

    GVA per filled job (£)

    Gateshead vs the North East average and the UK.

    Relative to UK (UK = 100)

    Both productivity measures have drifted further below the UK average since the mid-2000s.

    Jobs & total hours worked

    Filled jobs (left axis) and total hours worked per week (right axis).

    The productivity gap, in money

    Benchmarking vs statistical neighbours

    Gateshead's ONS economic statistical nearest neighbours — the local authorities most similar on economic structure — alongside the North East and the UK. This is the fairest like-for-like comparison.

    Productivity landscape

    GVA per hour (x) vs GVA per filled job (y), latest year. Gateshead highlighted; UK and NE average marked.

    GVA per job vs neighbours

    £ per filled job, latest year. Dashed line = UK average.

    Statistical-neighbour scorecard

    Click a column header to sort. Gateshead highlighted.

    National & regional context

    Where Gateshead sits among all 360+ UK local authorities, where the fastest growth is, and how the borough's industry structure compares with the nation.

    Where Gateshead sits nationally

    Distribution of GVA per filled job across all UK local authorities. Gateshead marked.

    Where the real growth is

    Fastest- and slowest-growing local authorities, real GVA, last decade.

    UK local authority league table

    About this dashboard

    The 2024 data update

    This dashboard is built so the imminent 2024 figures drop straight in. GVA and productivity currently run to 2023; ONS refreshes balanced GVA (spring) and subregional productivity (summer) annually, so 2024 is expected mid-2026. Business counts (UKBC) already run to 2025 and employment (BRES) to 2024. Every chart reads its latest year from the data, and headline text is generated from it — rebuilding with a new vintage requires no design changes.

    Metrics

    • GVA, current prices — cash value added by industry (£m), includes inflation; best for comparing areas in a single year.
    • GVA, chained volume measures (CVM) — real value added in 2022 money; best for tracking growth over time.
    • GVA per hour worked — output per hour, smoothed, current price; the cleanest productivity measure.
    • GVA per filled job — output per job, smoothed, current price; reflects hours and job mix as well as efficiency.

    Data sources

      Statistical neighbours

      Notes & caveats

      • Current-price growth mixes real growth and inflation — do not read nominal GVA gains as real-terms gains.
      • Regional and national GVA aggregates here are summed from local-authority building blocks; CVM money values are summed on ONS's 2022 reference-year basis.
      • Sector value-per-job divides ONS GVA by BRES employee jobs; BRES counts employees (Great Britain) and excludes most self-employment, so levels are indicative, best read as a cross-sector comparison rather than a precise wage-style figure.
      • Productivity figures are ONS smoothed current-price estimates, recommended by ONS for subregional analysis; small areas can still be volatile.
      • The UK = 100 index is relative: a falling index can still coincide with rising absolute productivity if the UK grew faster.
      Open data from ONS and NOMIS. Built for Gateshead economic intelligence. Part of the Economic Intelligence catalogue · source.
